title: Revenue Quality and Profit Margins
objectives: Distinguish between high-quality and low-quality revenue streams
Analyse gross margin trends to assess competitive positioning
Identify operating leverage and its effect on profit growth

vocabulary.definition: Revenue that automatically renews and is highly predictable — subscriptions, SaaS fees, long-term contracts. More valuable than one-off transactional revenue.
Gross profit as a percentage of revenue. High and stable gross margins suggest pricing power and competitive advantage.
EBITDA as a percentage of revenue. A measure of operational profitability before non-cash charges and financing. Widely used for comparing companies across capital structures.
When a large portion of costs are fixed, each additional dollar of revenue generates a high proportion of additional profit — small revenue increases lead to large profit increases.
Total revenue divided by number of employees. High revenue per employee indicates a high-quality, efficient business model — particularly important for comparing service businesses.

activities.title: Not All Revenue Is Equal
activities.notePrompt: Rank these revenue types from most to least valuable, and explain why: one-off project fee, monthly subscription, government contract renewed annually, spot transaction (day-by-day), 5-year take-or-pay contract.
activities.content:
A company that earns $10 million from subscriptions is worth more than one earning $10 million from one-off project work — even if the profit margins are identical. Why? Predictability and retention.

Revenue quality spectrum (best to worst):

1. Multi-year contracted recurring revenue: Long-term take-or-pay contracts where the customer commits to purchase regardless. Example: Meridian Energy's long-term electricity contracts with large industrial customers. Highly predictable, bankable by lenders.

2. Subscription revenue: Customers pay regularly (monthly, annually) and must actively cancel to stop. SaaS software, streaming, mobile phone plans. High retention rates (85–95%+ per year). Predictable and expandable.

3. Government contracts: Often renewed, large, and the counterparty is low default risk. Valuable but can involve public tender processes.

4. Repeat transactional revenue: Customers return regularly but are not contractually committed. Supermarkets, fuel stations — high repeat rate due to habit and location, not contract.

5. One-off project or transactional revenue: Revenue that must be earned fresh each period. No guarantee of repetition. NZ example: a construction company's project revenue — each project must be won competitively.

6. Cyclical spot revenue: Priced daily in commodity markets. Extremely volatile. Fonterra's commodity dairy powder prices fluctuate with global supply and demand.

For analysis: check what percentage of revenue is recurring or under long-term contract. For FPH, the razor-and-blade model (sell consumables to hospitals that have already purchased the hardware) creates highly recurring consumable revenue.

activities.title: Operating Leverage — How Revenue Growth Turbocharges Profits
activities.notePrompt: Company A has $100M revenue, $60M fixed costs and $20M variable costs. Revenue grows 20% to $120M. Variable costs grow proportionally. Calculate: what happens to profit? What is the 'profit leverage ratio' (% profit growth ÷ % revenue growth)?
activities.content:
Operating leverage is one of the most powerful concepts in business analysis. It explains why some companies' profits grow much faster than their revenues.

How it works: Companies have fixed costs (the same regardless of revenue) and variable costs (that grow proportionally with revenue). Fixed costs include rent, management salaries, software licences, depreciation.

Example:
Revenue: $100M | Fixed costs: $60M | Variable costs (20% of revenue): $20M | Profit: $20M

Revenue grows 20% to $120M:
Fixed costs: still $60M (unchanged)
Variable costs: 20% of $120M = $24M
New profit: $120M − $60M − $24M = $36M
Profit growth: ($36M − $20M) ÷ $20M × 100 = 80%

Revenue grew 20% but profit grew 80% — a 4× leverage ratio. This is operating leverage in action.

Why it matters for investors: Companies with high fixed cost bases AND growing revenue compound earnings very rapidly. Software companies (once built, distribution costs are minimal), healthcare device companies (FPH), and infrastructure businesses all demonstrate strong operating leverage.

The flip side: operating leverage works in reverse too. Revenue falling 20% could reduce profit by 80% for the same business. High fixed costs amplify downturns as much as they amplify growth — which is why companies with high operating leverage are more volatile.

activities.statement: A company with 80% recurring revenue is always better than one with 40% recurring revenue, regardless of the gross margin on each revenue type.
activities.explanation: Not necessarily. Recurring revenue is more predictable and valuable — but if the recurring revenue has a very low gross margin (say 5%) while the non-recurring revenue has a 60% margin, a pure comparison of recurring revenue percentages misses the picture. For example, a company with $100M of 5% margin subscriptions and $60M of 55% margin project work might generate less total profit than a company with 40% recurring revenue at high margins. Always assess both revenue quality (predictability) AND revenue profitability (margin) together.

activities.title: Analysing Gross Margin Trends
activities.notePrompt: FPH's gross margins over 5 years: 54.2%, 55.8%, 57.1%, 55.4%, 56.9%. What trend do you observe? What might cause the dip in year 4? What does the overall trend tell you about FPH's competitive position?
activities.content:
Gross margin trends tell you more about competitive positioning than the absolute level.

A stable or rising gross margin is evidence of sustained pricing power — the company can maintain or improve the premium customers pay relative to cost of goods.

A declining gross margin signals competitive pressure, commodity input cost increases the company cannot pass through to customers, or product mix shift toward lower-margin items.

FPH gross margin analysis (2019–2024):
2019: 54.2% → 2020: 55.8% → 2021: 57.1% → 2022: 55.4% → 2023: 56.9% → 2024: 57.8%

Observations: The overall trend is upward (+3.6 percentage points over 5 years). The dip in 2022 coincided with the COVID recovery period when supply chain costs rose globally for medical device manufacturers — a temporary external factor, not competitive erosion. The return to 56.9% in 2023 and 57.8% in 2024 confirms the dip was transient. The sustained upward trend over 5 years is a hallmark of widening competitive advantage — FPH is gaining pricing power over time.

Spark NZ gross margin trend for comparison:
2019: 44.1% → 2020: 43.2% → 2021: 42.5% → 2022: 41.8% → 2023: 41.2%

This steady decline tells a different story — competitive pressure from rivals is progressively compressing Spark's ability to earn margin on its services. This is the numerical evidence of Spark's narrowing moat.

questions.question: A software company has $80M revenue, $60M in fixed costs and $8M in variable costs, earning $12M profit. Revenue grows 25% to $100M. What is the new profit and the profit growth rate?
options: Profit grows 25% to $15M — profit always grows proportionally with revenue
Profit grows to $32M — a 167% increase, demonstrating operating leverage from fixed-cost base
Profit grows to $22M — a simple application of 25% growth to the existing $12M
Profit stays at $12M — fixed costs are fixed so profit cannot grow
questions.explanation: New revenue: $100M. Variable costs grow 25%: $8M × 1.25 = $10M. Fixed costs: still $60M. New profit: $100M − $60M − $10M = $30M. Profit growth: ($30M − $12M) ÷ $12M × 100 = 150%. Approximately 6× leverage (150% profit growth ÷ 25% revenue growth). This dramatic operating leverage comes from the $60M fixed cost base — those costs are already covered, so each additional revenue dollar mostly flows to profit.

questions.question: A company's gross margin has declined from 45% to 36% over 5 years while revenue grew 15% per year. What does this most likely indicate?
options: The company is successfully growing by competing on price — low margins are a strategic choice
Input cost increases or competitive pressure have eroded pricing power — the company must spend more to generate each dollar of gross profit, potentially threatening long-term profitability
Gross margin decline is irrelevant if revenue is growing — what matters is absolute profit growth
This pattern is consistent with an improving moat — growing companies always see margin compression
questions.explanation: A 9 percentage point gross margin decline over 5 years is significant even if revenue is growing. It means the company is generating less profit per dollar of sales over time — either competitors are forcing price cuts, or input costs are rising faster than the company can pass through to customers. Revenue growth can mask this problem short-term, but if the trend continues, eventual profit growth will stall even with continued top-line growth. This is a moat erosion warning sign.

activities.title: High or Low Revenue Quality? Sort These Revenue Types

pairs.item: Monthly software subscription fees — cancellable but historically 92% renewal rate
One-off construction project fee — must win competitive tender each time
5-year take-or-pay electricity contract with a large industrial customer
Daily commodity spot sales — price resets to global market every morning
Annual government maintenance contract renewed for 8 consecutive years
Tourist revenue — highly seasonal and cancelled entirely during COVID

activities.explanation: High-quality revenue is predictable, recurring and sticky. Low-quality revenue must be earned fresh each period, is cyclical or competitively bid. Companies with predominantly high-quality revenue deserve premium valuations because investors can model future earnings with more confidence. This difference in revenue quality is a major reason SaaS software companies trade at higher P/E multiples than construction companies.

activities.statement: Operating leverage means a company's profits will always grow faster than its revenue, making companies with high fixed costs always better investments.
activities.explanation: False. Operating leverage is symmetrical — it amplifies both growth and decline. When revenue grows, profits grow faster (positive leverage). When revenue falls, profits fall even faster (negative leverage). This makes high-fixed-cost businesses with operating leverage more volatile — excellent in bull phases, more painful in downturns. Airlines, for example, have very high fixed costs (aircraft, crew, slots) and show extreme operating leverage — in good years they are very profitable; in downturns they burn cash rapidly.

activities.question: A company has $50M revenue and $35M in fixed costs with $10M variable costs, earning $5M profit. If revenue grows 40% to $70M and variable costs grow proportionally, what is the new profit? Enter the dollar amount.
activities.hint: Fixed costs stay at $35M. Variable costs grow 40% from $10M. New profit = new revenue minus fixed costs minus new variable costs.
activities.explanation: New revenue: $70M. Variable costs: $10M × 1.40 = $14M. Fixed costs: $35M (unchanged). New profit: $70M − $35M − $14M = $21M. Profit grew from $5M to $21M — a 320% increase on 40% revenue growth. The leverage ratio is 320% ÷ 40% = 8×. The $35M fixed cost base means almost all of the $20M revenue increase flows to profit after the small variable cost increase.

activities.title: Revenue Quality Assessment
activities.prompt: Think about any company you interact with daily. Estimate what percentage of its revenue is recurring (subscription, regular repeat) versus one-off or transactional. Does this revenue quality reflect in how confident you would feel about its earnings next year? Write three sentences.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — revenue quality analysis is one of the most underappreciated skills in equity analysis.
